content.problemStatement:
You are given two matrices AAA and BBB, each with RRR rows (numbered 111 through RRR) and CCC columns (numbered 111 through CCC). Let's denote an element of AAA or BBB in row iii and column jjj by Ai,jA_{i,j}Ai,j​ or Bi,jB_{i,j}Bi,j​ respectively.

You are also given an integer XXX. You may perform the following operation on AAA any number of times:


Choose an integer vvv.
Choose XXX consecutive elements of AAA, either in the same row or in the same column.
Add vvv to each of the chosen elements of AAA.
Determine whether it is possible to change AAA to BBB in a finite number of operations.
constraints: Sum of R over all test cases <= 10^3
Sum of C over all test cases <= 10^3
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains three integers R, C and X. Then R lines follow, each containing C integers describing matrix A. After that, another R lines follow, each containing C integers describing matrix B.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "Yes" if it is possible to transform A into B using the allowed operations, otherwise output "No".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: sum of differences = -2, which is divisible by X=2, so answer is Yes.\nTest case 2: sum of differences = -1, not divisible by 2, answer No.\nTest case 3: sum of differences = -3, not divisible by 2, answer No.

hidden.label: odd total sum with even X

solution.approach: Let D[i][j] = B[i][j] - A[i][j]. Each operation adds the same integer v to exactly X cells, therefore the total sum of all matrix elements changes by a multiple of X. Consequently, the sum of all D[i][j] must be divisible by X; otherwise the transformation is impossible. This condition is also sufficient: we can greedily eliminate differences row‑wise and column‑wise. For each row i, for columns j = 0 … C‑X, apply an operation on the segment (i, j … j+X‑1) with v = D[i][j]; this makes D[i][j] zero. After processing all rows, only the last X‑1 columns may contain non‑zero values. Then process columns similarly for rows i = 0 … R‑X, using column segments of length X to zero the remaining cells. The remaining bottom‑right (X‑1)×(X‑1) sub‑matrix automatically satisfies the sum‑divisible condition, so it can be cleared by combining row and column operations. Hence the only required check is sum(D) % X == 0. The algorithm runs in O(R·C) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(R * C) per test case, dominated by scanning the matrices once.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra besides the input matrices (differences are accumulated on the fly).

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        R = int(next(it)); C = int(next(it)); X = int(next(it))
        A = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(R*C)]
        sum_diff = 0
        for i in range(R*C):
            b = int(next(it))
            sum_diff += b - A[i]
        out.append('Yes' if sum_diff % X == 0 else 'No')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
